tasks.description: Identify and classify a KOL's tier based on their profile
tasks.input: A crypto influencer has 75,000 Twitter followers, posts 3-4 times daily about Solana DeFi, gets 200-400 likes per tweet and 20-50 replies, has been active for 2 years, and promoted 4 projects in the last month. Two of those projects are still active and growing. What tier is this KOL, and would you recommend them for a Solana DeFi protocol launch?

scoring.5: Correctly identifies as micro KOL (10K-100K range); notes the engagement rate is healthy (0.3-0.5% reply rate, 3-5% like rate relative to followers); recognizes Solana DeFi specialization as a strength
scoring.3: Correctly identifies tier but doesn't calculate or assess engagement rate

scoring.5: Provides a qualified recommendation considering: Solana DeFi alignment (strong positive), healthy engagement rate, reasonable promotion frequency (4/month is borderline but acceptable), track record of promoted projects surviving; flags items to verify (fake follower check, audience overlap with existing community)
scoring.3: Gives a recommendation but misses key factors or doesn't qualify it

scoring.5: Flags: 4 promotions/month is on the higher end (should verify each was genuinely endorsed vs paid shill); should check if the 2 inactive projects rugged; should verify engagement is organic; assess if audience trusts their project recommendations
scoring.3: Mentions 1-2 risk factors but misses others

tasks.description: Calculate fair pricing for a KOL engagement
tasks.input: A macro KOL with 250K Twitter followers and a 3.5% engagement rate is quoting $12,000 for a single Twitter thread (7 tweets) about our token launch. They also have a YouTube channel with 80K subscribers. Is this price fair? What counter-offer would you suggest, and what payment structure would you recommend?

scoring.5: Assesses against macro tier benchmark ($3K-$15K for a campaign); notes $12K for a single thread is on the high end; considers the YouTube channel as additional leverage for negotiation (could bundle); analyzes cost-per-engagement based on 3.5% rate on 250K followers
scoring.3: Says the price is high or fair but doesn't reference benchmarks or do the math

scoring.5: Suggests a counter-offer that includes: lower base fee ($5K-$8K), bundled YouTube video at a discount, performance bonus tied to KPIs, token allocation with vesting; explains the rationale for each component
scoring.3: Suggests a lower price but doesn't structure a comprehensive counter-offer

scoring.5: Recommends milestone-based payment (30/40/30 split); includes vesting for any token component; ties final payment to deliverable verification; mentions performance bonus structure
scoring.3: Mentions milestone payments but structure is incomplete
scoring.1: Suggests lump-sum or vague payment terms

tasks.description: Identify fake followers from engagement data
tasks.input: We're considering a KOL who claims 180K followers on X. Here's their recent data: average 5,000 likes per tweet but only 8 replies, follower growth spiked 40% in one week last month (from 130K to 180K) with no viral content during that period, 45% of followers have accounts less than 3 months old, and their audience is 60% from Southeast Asia despite posting only in English about Ethereum DeFi. Is this KOL legitimate?

scoring.5: Identifies all 4 major red flags: (1) extreme like-to-reply ratio (625:1, far above 50:1 threshold), (2) 40% follower spike without viral content = purchased followers, (3) 45% young accounts = bot cohort, (4) 60% SEA audience mismatch with English Ethereum DeFi content; concludes this is almost certainly a bot-inflated account
scoring.3: Identifies 2-3 red flags but misses others or doesn't quantify thresholds

scoring.5: Strongly recommends against hiring; estimates real organic following is likely 80K-100K (pre-spike level minus bot decay); suggests what additional verification to run if the team still wants to consider them
scoring.3: Recommends caution but doesn't quantify the risk or estimate real following

scoring.5: Suggests specific next steps: run Social Blade analysis on follower growth timeline, sample 30 followers manually, check if the spike coincides with a known bot network operation, compare engagement before and after the spike
scoring.3: Mentions 'further investigation' without specific steps

tasks.description: Plan a multi-platform KOL campaign for a token launch
tasks.input: We're launching a GameFi token on Arbitrum in 6 weeks. Budget is $50,000. The game has 5,000 active players and 8K Twitter followers. We want to grow to 50K followers and 20K active players by launch. The game combines on-chain PvP battles with an NFT marketplace. Target audience: crypto gamers, NFT collectors, and Arbitrum ecosystem users. Plan a complete multi-platform KOL campaign with specific deliverables, timeline, and budget allocation.

scoring.5: Proposes a specific tier mix (e.g., 1 mega/2 macro/5 micro/15 nano) with dollar amounts per tier; justifies the mix for GameFi (gaming YouTubers are crucial, Telegram degen groups for NFT audience, Arbitrum-specific accounts); budget allocation table with platform breakdown; reserves budget for tracking tools and content production
scoring.3: Has tier mix and some budget numbers but allocation rationale is weak or misses a key audience segment
scoring.1: Generic budget split without GameFi-specific rationale

scoring.5: 6-week timeline organized in waves aligned with pre-launch milestones; Wave 1 (weeks 1-2): micro KOL seeding + gameplay demos; Wave 2 (weeks 3-4): macro KOL amplification + YouTube reviews; Wave 3 (weeks 5-6): mega KOL + NFT influencers for launch hype; post-launch sustain plan; specific dates/milestones per wave
scoring.3: Has a timeline with some wave structure but milestones are vague or waves aren't differentiated enough

scoring.5: Specifies content for each platform: X (gameplay clips, alpha threads, space with devs), YouTube (gameplay reviews, NFT marketplace walkthrough, 'play to earn' analysis), Telegram (alpha group posts, gameplay community), Discord (partner server AMAs); each platform targets a different audience segment; cross-platform synergy described
scoring.3: Multi-platform mentioned but content types are generic or same content pushed everywhere

scoring.5: Includes GameFi-specific campaign elements: gameplay demo streams, tournament with KOL participation, NFT giveaways through KOL channels, early access codes for KOL communities, guild/clan partnership strategy, 'earn while reviewing' model for KOLs; understands that GameFi audiences need to SEE gameplay not just read about it
scoring.3: Some GameFi awareness but mostly generic crypto marketing tactics applied to a game
scoring.1: Treats this like a standard token launch with no gaming context

tasks.description: Detect fake influence and recommend alternatives
tasks.input:
Our marketing team has shortlisted 3 KOLs for a DeFi campaign. Analyze each and recommend which to hire:

KOL A: 320K followers, 2.1% engagement rate, posts about DeFi/trading, promoted 12 projects last month, all still active. Charges $8,000/thread.

KOL B: 45K followers, 7.8% engagement rate, deep Ethereum DeFi specialist, promoted 2 projects last month (both top DeFi protocols), runs a popular analytics dashboard. Charges $2,500/thread.

KOL C: 890K followers, 0.8% engagement rate, general crypto content, promoted 25 projects last month, 8 of which have rugged. Charges $25,000/thread. Claims massive reach justifies the price.

scoring.5: Analyzes each KOL with specific reasoning: KOL A — macro tier, decent engagement but 12 promotions/month is a volume shill concern; KOL B — micro tier, exceptional engagement rate, specialist credibility, selective promotion history is a trust signal; KOL C — mega tier but 0.8% engagement is below floor, 25 promos/month is a red flag factory, 8 rugged projects is disqualifying; includes cost-per-engagement calculations
scoring.3: Analyzes each KOL but misses key red flags or doesn't compare quantitatively
scoring.1: Surface-level analysis without specific metrics evaluation

scoring.5: Clearly recommends KOL B as the top pick with detailed justification (best engagement-to-cost ratio, DeFi specialist, selective = trusted, analytics dashboard gives additional credibility); ranks KOL A as acceptable with conditions (negotiate lower price, limit to 1 thread not a campaign); strongly rejects KOL C with specific disqualification reasons; calculates ROI comparison
scoring.3: Correct ranking but justification is incomplete or doesn't quantify the value difference

scoring.5: For KOL C: 0.8% engagement on 890K followers suggests massive fake following; 25 promos/month = pure pay-to-shill; 8/25 rugged = 32% rug rate is reputation poison; $25K price is 3x overvalued based on real engagement; hiring this KOL would actively harm project credibility
scoring.3: Identifies KOL C as problematic but doesn't quantify the severity
scoring.1: Mentions concerns about KOL C but doesn't strongly recommend against

scoring.5: Provides negotiation strategy for recommended KOL(s): offer KOL B a premium ($3K-$3.5K) for exclusivity + YouTube video bundle; for KOL A, negotiate down to $5K-$6K with performance bonus; suggests payment structure for each
scoring.3: Some negotiation advice but not specific to each KOL

tasks.description: Design a KOL campaign for a bear market context
tasks.input: We're a well-funded DeFi protocol (series A, $5M in treasury) but we're launching in a bear market. Twitter engagement is down 60% across crypto, KOL prices have dropped 40%, many KOLs have lost credibility from bull market shilling. Budget is $30,000 for a 2-month campaign. How should we approach KOL marketing differently in a bear market? What opportunities does a bear market create?

scoring.5: Identifies specific bear market dynamics: lower KOL prices = more KOLs per dollar, quality KOLs still posting = genuinely passionate, reduced noise = easier to stand out, audience is more sophisticated (tourists left), focus shifts from hype to substance/utility; reframes bear market as an opportunity not a limitation
scoring.3: Acknowledges bear market changes some things but doesn't deeply analyze opportunities
scoring.1: Applies bull market playbook with minor tweaks

scoring.5: Recommends prioritizing: KOLs who maintained posting frequency through bear (true believers), analysts/researchers over hype creators, KOLs who lost followers but maintained engagement rate (organic audience stayed), builders who run their own projects; explicitly avoids: KOLs who went silent (will return when bull comes), KOLs still shilling everything (desperation signals)
scoring.3: Adjusts KOL selection somewhat but doesn't articulate bear-specific criteria clearly
scoring.1: Same selection criteria as bull market

scoring.5: Bear market content strategy: lead with utility/revenue/real yield not token price; focus on technical deep dives and educational content; avoid hype language (triggers bear market cynicism); leverage 'building in the bear' narrative; position as serious protocol that launches when ready not when market is hot; suggests specific content formats (audit reviews, yield comparisons, risk analysis)
scoring.3: Some messaging adjustment but still includes hype elements inappropriate for bear market
scoring.1: Standard promotional messaging regardless of market

scoring.5: Leverages bear market pricing: negotiate 40-50% below stated rates, lock in multi-month ambassador deals while prices are low, heavy micro/nano allocation (their bear market prices are extremely attractive), reserve budget for bull market re-activation; calculates that $30K in bear buys equivalent of $50K+ in bull market reach
scoring.3: Some budget optimization awareness but doesn't fully exploit bear market pricing
scoring.1: Standard budget allocation without market consideration

tasks.description: Create a KOL deliverable tracking system and contract template
tasks.input: We're about to run our first large-scale KOL campaign with 15 KOLs across 3 tiers. I need: (1) a deliverable tracking framework that works for 15 simultaneous KOLs, (2) key contract terms to protect us from common KOL problems (ghost, fake engagement, competing promotions), and (3) an escalation protocol when things go wrong. Make it practical — we're a 3-person marketing team.

scoring.5: Provides a practical tracking system: dashboard structure with KOL name, tier, deliverables, due dates, status, links, metrics columns; daily monitoring routine for a 3-person team (assign KOLs per person); automated tracking suggestions (UTM links, referral codes, social listening tools); weekly summary report template; flags what to monitor in real-time vs batch
scoring.3: Has a tracking structure but doesn't account for the 3-person team constraint or daily workflow

scoring.5: Covers all critical contract terms: milestone-based payment (30/40/30), content approval clause, disclosure compliance requirement, exclusivity window, revision policy, kill clause, clawback for non-delivery, fake engagement penalty, minimum engagement guarantee, IP/usage rights, confidentiality; explains WHY each term matters with examples of what goes wrong without it
scoring.3: Covers payment terms and basic deliverables but misses protective clauses
scoring.1: Generic contract advice without KOL-specific terms

scoring.5: Tiered escalation: Level 1 (minor delay/quality) — DM reminder, note in tracker; Level 2 (>48h delay, content deviation) — formal notice, withhold payment; Level 3 (ghost, fake engagement, breach) — terminate contract, invoke clawback, document for KOL database; includes communication templates and decision trees for common scenarios
scoring.3: Has escalation steps but not tiered or lacks specific actions per level
scoring.1: Mentions escalation without a structured protocol

scoring.5: Explicitly designs for a 3-person team: assigns roles (1 person manages macro, 1 manages micro, 1 manages nano+admin); suggests tool stack (Notion/Sheets for tracking, Hootsuite for monitoring, Bitly for link tracking); estimates daily time investment per person; identifies what to automate vs manual review
scoring.3: Acknowledges team size but doesn't optimize the system for it

tasks.description: Design a KOL strategy for a controversial token launch with regulatory sensitivity
tasks.input: We're launching a privacy-focused DeFi protocol with a token that enables private transactions on Ethereum. We expect regulatory scrutiny (similar to Tornado Cash concerns). Our protocol is legal and compliant (has OFAC screening), but the 'privacy' narrative triggers FUD. Budget is $80,000 for a 3-month campaign. Design a KOL strategy that builds genuine community while navigating the regulatory and narrative minefields. How do we select KOLs who won't run away when the FUD hits? How do we pre-empt regulatory narratives?

scoring.5: Develops a comprehensive narrative strategy: frame as 'financial privacy' not 'anonymous transactions', lead with compliance features (OFAC screening), position alongside existing privacy standards (HIPAA, GDPR), pre-empt Tornado Cash comparisons with clear differentiators, prepare a FUD response playbook, engage compliance-focused KOLs and legal commentators as credibility anchors; understands the narrative battle is as important as the marketing campaign
scoring.3: Acknowledges the regulatory challenge but narrative strategy is thin or reactive rather than proactive
scoring.1: Ignores the regulatory dimension and runs a standard campaign
scoring.0: Narrative strategy would increase regulatory risk

scoring.5: Selects KOL archetypes specifically suited for controversy: privacy advocates with principled track records, legal/compliance commentators in crypto, cypherpunk community leaders, Ethereum core community voices who support privacy; avoids: KOLs who will delete posts at first sign of controversy, KOLs with SEC sensitivity (US-based large influencers), KOLs who promoted Tornado Cash forks without nuance; screens for 'spine' — will they stand behind the promotion when challenged?
scoring.3: Some selection criteria acknowledge the controversy but doesn't deeply screen for resilience
scoring.1: Standard KOL selection with no controversy consideration
scoring.0: Selects KOLs likely to amplify negative narratives

scoring.5: Phase 1 (Month 1): Education campaign — KOL analysts explain why privacy matters in DeFi (no token mention yet); Phase 2 (Month 2): Credibility building — compliance-focused KOLs review the OFAC screening mechanism, legal commentators assess regulatory positioning; Phase 3 (Month 3): Launch — broader KOL campaign with the credibility foundation already laid; includes crisis management protocol for each phase; reserves 20% of budget for rapid-response if FUD campaign emerges
scoring.3: Multi-phase but doesn't build the narrative foundation before launch promotion

scoring.5: Pre-built FUD response framework: prepared counter-narratives for predictable attacks (Tornado Cash comparison, 'criminal tool', regulatory shutdown), designated KOL rapid-response team (3-5 KOLs contracted for on-call FUD response), pre-drafted response threads that KOLs can adapt, monitoring system for emerging negative narratives, legal review of all crisis responses before publication
scoring.3: Acknowledges FUD risk but crisis plan is reactive not pre-built
scoring.1: Mentions FUD without a management plan

tasks.description: Optimize ROI across a failing multi-KOL campaign mid-flight
tasks.input:
We're 2 weeks into a 4-week KOL campaign for our DEX aggregator on Solana. Budget was $40,000, we've spent $25,000 so far. Results are terrible:
- Mega KOL ($12K): Posted once, got 500K impressions but only 15 replies and 0 referral link clicks. Hasn't posted the 2nd contracted thread.
- Macro KOL A ($5K): Posted 3 threads, decent engagement (200+ replies each), but referral link generated only 45 wallet connections.
- Macro KOL B ($4K): Ghosted after receiving upfront payment. No posts delivered.
- 5 Micro KOLs ($800 each = $4K): 3 have posted with mediocre results (20-40 replies each). 2 haven't posted yet.

We have $15,000 remaining and 2 weeks left. How do we salvage this campaign? What went wrong, and how should we reallocate the remaining budget?

scoring.5: Diagnoses each KOL's failure specifically: Mega KOL — 500K impressions with 15 replies = bot-inflated reach, 0 link clicks = audience has zero intent, overpaid massively; Macro A — engagement is real but 45 conversions from 600+ replies = messaging or product-market issue, not KOL issue; Macro B — ghosted = no milestone payments used, upfront payment mistake; Micros — mediocre results suggest brief was weak or KOLs not aligned with Solana DEX audience; identifies the root cause distinction: KOL selection problem vs messaging problem vs product problem
scoring.3: Identifies some issues per KOL but doesn't distinguish between selection, messaging, and product problems
scoring.1: Generic 'campaign isn't working' without specific diagnosis

scoring.5: Immediate steps: (1) Invoke contract clause with Mega KOL — demand 2nd thread or partial refund; (2) Document Macro B ghost, send formal notice, attempt clawback; (3) Analyze Macro A's 45 conversions — what's the drop-off? Is the landing page/onboarding broken? (4) Contact 2 undelivered micro KOLs — deliver or terminate; (5) Audit referral link tracking — is it working correctly? This is the most actionable analysis.
scoring.3: Some immediate actions but misses key recovery steps

scoring.5: With $15K remaining: (1) If Macro A's conversion issue is messaging-based, reallocate $5K to create better content/landing page and have Macro A repost with improved angle; (2) Shift $6-8K to 8-10 new micro KOLs specifically in Solana DEX/aggregator niche (the original micro selection was too generic); (3) Reserve $2-3K for a Solana Twitter Space featuring Macro A + top micro KOLs (interactive format for conversion); (4) Kill the mega KOL model — it clearly doesn't work for a DEX aggregator; provides specific expected ROI improvement for each reallocation
scoring.3: Reallocates budget but rationale is thin or doesn't learn from what failed
scoring.1: Suggests spending remaining $15K the same way as first $25K

scoring.5: Extracts actionable lessons: (1) Never use mega KOLs for utility products (DEX aggregators need targeted, not broad reach); (2) Always use milestone payments (Macro B ghost was preventable); (3) Test messaging with 1-2 KOLs before scaling to 10; (4) Track referral link functionality before campaign starts; (5) Match KOL niche to product type (DEX aggregator needs trading-focused KOLs, not general crypto); recommends process changes for the next campaign
scoring.3: Some lessons but doesn't connect them to structural process improvements

tasks.description: Design a cross-regional KOL expansion strategy for emerging markets
tasks.input: Our DeFi protocol has strong traction in the US/EU market (50K users, $100M TVL). We want to expand into Southeast Asia (Vietnam, Philippines, Indonesia), South Korea, and Turkey — regions with high crypto adoption but different KOL ecosystems, languages, and platforms. Budget is $100,000 for a 3-month expansion campaign. Design a multi-regional KOL strategy that accounts for cultural differences, local platforms, language barriers, and regional KOL dynamics. We have no local team members.

scoring.5: Demonstrates understanding of each region's KOL landscape: SEA (YouTube-heavy, Telegram groups dominant, local language essential, price-sensitive audience), Korea (KakaoTalk, Naver, Korean crypto YouTube, relationship-heavy business culture, Korean language required), Turkey (high inflation drives crypto adoption, Turkish language Twitter CT, Telegram dominant, local exchanges are cultural centers); identifies region-specific platforms beyond Twitter; addresses the 'no local team' challenge with solutions (regional KOL agencies, bilingual community managers, local ambassador leads)
scoring.3: Acknowledges regional differences but analysis is surface-level or misses key platforms
scoring.1: Applies Western KOL strategy to all regions

scoring.5: Allocates budget proportionally to opportunity and cost: SEA might get 35% ($35K — 3 countries, large population, lower KOL costs), Korea 30% ($30K — premium market, higher KOL costs, need local agency), Turkey 20% ($20K — single country, high adoption, moderate costs); reserves 15% ($15K) for tracking tools, translations, and contingency; justifies allocation with market-size and cost-per-KOL data per region
scoring.3: Has regional budget split but rationale is arbitrary or doesn't account for cost differences
scoring.1: Equal split or no regional budget breakdown

scoring.5: Addresses localization deeply: all content must be in local language (not English with subtitles); KOLs should create original local-language content not translate English posts; cultural messaging adaptation (SEA: emphasis on earning/income, Korea: emphasis on innovation/tech sophistication, Turkey: emphasis on inflation hedge/savings); local platform strategy (YouTube Shorts for SEA, Naver Blog for Korea); recommends hiring regional KOL managers or agencies for each market
scoring.3: Mentions translation but doesn't address cultural messaging differences or local platform dynamics

scoring.5: Practical solutions for remote management: hire regional KOL agencies with crypto experience (specific agency types recommended), recruit 1 bilingual community lead per region from existing community, use established KOL networks (like Lunar Strategy for Korea, YGG for SEA gaming community), set up regional Telegram groups with local moderators, define quality control process despite language barriers (engagement metrics are universal, use bilingual reviewers for content approval)
scoring.3: Some solutions for remote management but impractical or incomplete
